data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: THE DECISION RULE.
blocks: The central principle of this chapter is simple: the work chooses the tool, not the other way around.
The Decision Rule — This sounds obvious. It is regularly violated. Users choose the Claude surface that is closest at hand (chat) or the one

data.title: THE FIVE ROUTING DIMENSIONS.
blocks: What does success look like as an object?
Where does the necessary information live?
What is the cost of being wrong?
Can the action be undone?
How will you know the output is right?

data.title: THE ROUTING TABLE.
blocks: Brainstorm research angles for a grant proposal — Claude AI
Fix a failing test in a software repository — Claude Code
Organize and rename project files across a shared folder — Claude Cowork
Draft a follow-up email from notes pasted into chat — Claude AI

data.title: THE ROUTING DIAGRAM.
blocks: If you prefer a sequential process, here is the routing decision as a flow:
The Routing Diagram — What is the task output? → Text/prose/draft → Claude AI → Code/diff/script → Claude Code → File/report/folder operation

blocks: The task in full:

Step 2 — Context source.
Step 3 — Risk.
Step 4 — Reversibility.

The Human Gate — You ask the routing questions. You own the routing decision. Everything downstream depends on whether you asked them bef

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Asking "can Claude do this?" instead of "should Claude do this on this surface?"
Skipping the verification path question.
Treating reversibility as binary.
Using risk labels loosely.
Routing by familiarity.
